data.title: Hess Midstream: A Compelling Idea For 2026
data.description:
Hess Midstream (HESM) has been a poor performer over the past year, losing 8% of its value with particular weakness over the past three months. Concerns about slower long-term growth and Chevron’s (CVX) Bakken drilling plans have greatly dampened enthusiasm around HESM. I last covered Hess Midstream in September, reiterating the stock as a “strong buy” given my view investors had become too concerned about production growth in the Bakken. Since then, shares have lost about 2%, suggesting this call has been, at the least, early. With updated financials and 2026 coming into clearer view, now is a good time to revisit HESM. I view the pessimism as greatly overstated.

The weakness really began with CVX moving to a three-rig program in the Bakken from a four-rig program, reducing long-term production growth potential. As a result, HESM is pivoting away from focusing on growth, given more muted drilling by Chevron and is focusing on maximizing free cash flow for shareholder returns. Chevron is focused on sustaining oil production at about 200,000 barrels a day. I would note this fully protect HESM’s existing cash flow. This week, Chevron announced plans to spend $18 billion on cap-ex in 2026, at the low end of its $18-21 billion multiyear target, speaking to its focus on capital efficiency in this commodity price environment. About one-third of this budget will be spent on US shale plays, including the Bakken, which is most relevant to HESM’s results.

Now, I would emphasize that these fears are more about the future than the present. In the company’s third quarter reported on November 3rd, Hess Midstream earned $0.75, which beat estimates by $0.06. Revenue grew by 11% to $421 million, a reminder that while investors have become fearful about the future, existing results are still very strong.

The company now expects 2025 EBITDA of $1.245-1.255 billion within its prior $1.235-$1.285 billion range. At the midpoint, EBITDA is up 10% from last year. It cut its cap-ex outlook by another $30 million as it has suspended construction at the Capa gas plant and is walking away from the project. Free cash flow should be about $765 million. Sustaining cap-ex is about $125 million. Now some growth projects are underway, so in practice, I view minimum cap-ex as closer to $175-200 million, but this still provides room to reduce cap-ex further and improve free cash flow next year.

In Q3, HESM generated $321 million of EBITDA up from $287 million last year thanks to revenue growth and an expansion in margins to 82% from 81%. Adjusted free cash flow was $187 million up from $141 million last year given higher EBITDA and lower cap-ex as HESM ramps down growth spending. As you can see below, volumes across Hess’s system are still growing. Natural gas activity leads the way with processing growing over 10%.

While growth will slow, it is important to remember that HESM has a strong contract with Chevron (which inherited Hess’s role in the contract) that provides stable cash flow through 2033. Minimum volume commitments are set on a 3-year forward basis, and once set, they can never go down. They are set at 80% of anticipated production levels. Additionally, HESM’s fees rise over time, generally rising at CPI through the life of the contract. This provides structural revenue growth and insulates margins from potential inflation pressures.

Now, volumes across HESM’s system are running above MVC levels. While MVCs protect the vast majority of revenue, revenue could in theory fall from current levels if CVX production fell substantially. While I like to see a pipeline company have substantial MVC protection, I also do prefer to see volumes run above MVC levels. If volumes are below MVC levels, that points to long-term challenges with difficulty re-contracting when the contract matures whereas robust volumes speak to a durable model. In that way, MVC payments are more akin to a one-time windfall, as all contracts come to an end eventually, though HESM does have 8 more years on its contract with CVX, providing meaningful visibility.

Now, the stock currently has an 8.8% distribution yield, and management targets ongoing distribution growth of 5% through 2027. As reiterated on the earnings call, management can grow its distribution by 5% per year and cover it with cash flow, just from the MVCs. With volumes above their minimums this year, HESM should generate over $150 million of free cash flow after distributions this year. I expect about $50 million of free cash flow growth next year. That should enable about $200 million of buybacks beyond its distribution. That would reduce its unit count by 2-3%, essentially enabling half of its ~5% distribution/unit target without actually increasing the total cash cost of distributions.

During the quarter, the company bought back $100 million of stock. With where the stock is trading, I view buybacks as very accretive. With its equity yielding over 8%, one could even argue for debt-funded buybacks as the saved distribution costs would more than cover incremental cash interest expense. While I think debt financed buybacks could be justified, given the company’s conservative financial policy, I do not view them as likely. Buybacks will likely just be funded out of cash flow, and I do not expect the firm to consider increasing financial leverage. Leverage currently sits at 3x, which is consistent with HESM’s target.

I expect Chevron to proportionally participate in all future buybacks in order to maintain its ownership stake at the current 38% level. I saw Chevron’s investor day as a potential time for management to discuss its future plans for HESM. Instead, HESM received only passing mention with CVX simply acknowledging its 38% stake, and Chevron is not showing any signs of its next steps. I do not see a scenario where it would reduce its ownership stake as likely.

HESM should exit 2027 with an annualized distribution of about $3.33, giving the stock a 9.8% yield at the current price. Essentially, we do not need to see any cash flow growth beyond this point to provide investors a ~10% long-term return. In fact with the CPI riders, we could likely see 1-2% annual production declines beyond 2027 and still support the distribution.

This is why I view HESM as a compelling value opportunity. MVCs significantly truncate potential downside, and it does not even need any growth to provide a market-beating long-term return. With natural gas volumes likely to continue growing (especially as increasing electricity usage from data centers and export capacity support natural gas prices). This is also why longer term I view Chevron as likely to seek to acquire the 62% of HESM it does not own.

With a cost of debt closer to ~5%, this would be an immediately accretive acquisition while also simplifying its capital structure. I expect Chevron to be patient, and the longer the stock remains around $35, the more it may see a chance to successfully acquire HESM at closer to $40 than the $45 I once thought it would take to get majority support for a transaction. While timing is unknowable, I view CVX acquiring HESM as a distinct possibility in 2026. To be clear, I view HESM’s return prospects as attractive as a standalone entity with M&A premium just a sweetener to the bull case.

As such, I remain a “strong buy” on HESM and view it as an attractive long-term investment for income-oriented investors. Investors collect a nearly-9% yield, more than fully covered by free cash flow. Plus, ongoing distribution growth is fully covered by its MVCs. Its balance sheet is solid, and a multiyear return of 12-13% is my base case as I expect distribution growth to persist beyond 2027. A potential takeout by CVX could also accelerate returns. As investors grow more comfortable with underlying cash flow, even absent growth, I expect HESM to gradually move higher and towards $40. I rate it a “strong buy” and view HESM as one of the top values in the market.

data.title: Fund Your Retirement With High Yield Bonds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

High Yield Bonds offer higher returns with a little more risk than investment-grade bonds. It is critical, however, to examine the bond's default risk - credit ratings, ability to pay the interest due, and maturity length. This article summarizes my approach to select high yield bonds with superior returns and with acceptable risks. Bonds are assessed from a default risk perspective, and those with unacceptable risk of default are eliminated from consideration. The result is a recommended high yield bond portfolio with yield to maturities ranging from 5% to 34% with an average yield to maturity of 9%.

Background

I am a retired engineer. I have a diversified portfolio composed of equity funds, individual stocks, bond funds, and individual bonds. I invest in bonds and dividend stocks to provide the cash flow required to fund my retirement. I perform a quantitative analysis to select individual stocks and bonds.

Approximately 25% of my portfolio is in fixed income. Of the fixed income, 78% is in individual bonds, 16% in bond funds, and 6% in money market funds.

I recently published articles summarizing my individual stocks and equity and fixed income funds.

The article below discusses my individual bonds - selection criteria and recommended bonds.

High Yield Bonds Risk

High yield bonds offer higher returns with a little more risk than investment-grade bonds. The principal risk to high yield bonds is that the company issuing the bond defaults on them… declaring bankruptcy. Bonds do not go to zero value if the company declares bankruptcy. Bonds are senior in the credit order and must be made well before secured debt, preferred stock, or equity stock.

I have a personal example of the impact of company default on bond returns. About 5 years ago I bought a B rated bond that declared Chapter 11 a year after I bought it. The bond still traded on the market while the company was in chapter 11. I sold the bond for more than I paid for it. Including interest, my total gain was 34% on the bond. The second bond was a B- rated bond. After I bought the bond, its price deteriorated. I doubled down and bought more of the bonds. The company declared Chapter 11 2/3rds of a year after I bought the first bond. Its bonds still traded, however, on the stock market, and its price improved during the Chapter 11 reorganization process. I sold the bonds, and my total gains were 6% for the first bonds and 73% for the second. The last bond was a CCC+ rated bond that declared bankruptcy. I lost 78% on that bond. Overall, my average gain for all of the defaulted bonds was 34%. So default is not the end of the world for an individual bond holding. That said, I now do a more extensive assessment of bond default risk and have not had any bonds default using the selection methods I will summarize below.

Bond probability of default is a function of credit risk, ability to pay the interest, and time before the bond matures. Overall bond default rates as a function of credit rating and time are shown below. As you can see, longer maturity bonds have a higher risk of default than shorter maturity bonds.

Below is a plot of default probability versus credit ratings and time. Reduce default risk by picking high-yield bonds with better credit ratings and shorter time horizons.

One of my criteria for high yield bond selection is a maturity of 5 years or less. I want bonds that I can conceivably hold to maturity, which makes the total return independent of interest rate changes. And 5 years of less is a good maturity for minimizing default risk. BB credit rating is a good lower credit limit for purchasing high yield bonds. This article comes to the conclusion that BB default rates are negligibly higher than BBBs and significantly outperform BBBs.

High-Yield Bond Returns

High-yield bonds offer higher returns even with the greater risk of default than investment-grade bonds. This article says the return of bonds correlates to its initial yield to worse.

This chart from the article looks at bond returns over the last 35 years. Higher yield-to-worse bonds gave higher returns than lower yield-to-worse bonds even with their higher default risk.

The better long-term returns of high yield bonds versus investment grade bonds can be seen comparing two Vanguard bond funds.

The Vanguard High Yield fund has a higher yield and better long-term returns than their investment grade intermediate bond fund. The high yield fund is only a little more volatile than the investment-grade fund. In addition, the high yield fund dropped less during the COVID recession than the investment grade bond fund. The Vanguard High Yield fund contains about 1000 bonds. An individual investor can cherry pick a few high yield bonds that offer better returns than the Vanguard bond conglomerate will.

Bond Selection Criteria

I use the following criteria to select high yield bonds

  • Maturity 5 years of less
  • Credit ratings better than CCC
  • Close examination of the company Debt to EBITDA. Debt to EBITDA is a good indicator of the ability to pay interest. Debt/EBITDA 5 or less is ideal. A+ rated Toyota Debt/EBITDA is just under 5 (4.6). 10 is acceptable. Above ten acceptable if the yield to maturity is much higher than the YTM versus Debt/EBITDA trendline
  • Select bonds that have above average YTM versus credit risk than the trendline
  • Select bonds that have above average YTM versus Debt/EBITDA trendline

Bond Yield to Maturity versus Risk

I've examined a number of bonds. These include senior bonds available for sale on the Vanguard website as well as baby bonds available for sale on the stock exchange. Baby bonds have a credit priority just under senior bonds and secured debt. Baby bonds rank higher than preferred or equity stock. I've chosen the highest yielding bonds versus credit risk. The table below summarizes the best high yield bonds I've analyzed. The table lists the company, bond symbol, bond type, CUSPID for senior bonds, bond credit rating, coupon, maturity date, Debt to EBITDA, current yield, Yield to maturity, and rate of default for bonds that have a credit rating. Credit ratings come from S&P Global Ratings, Moody's, and Egan-Jones rating of baby bonds when initially issued.

A plot of Yield to maturity versus credit rating is shown below.

A plot of Yield to maturity versus credit rating is shown below.

I currently own the bonds labeled in blue. In addition, I have a large position (23% of individual bond portfolio) in Treasury Bonds. I may adjust my bond portfolio based on this analysis.

Recommended High Yield Bonds

The bonds below are my current recommended individual high yield bond portfolio. I eliminated bonds that either had too high a credit or Debt/EBITDA risk. I selected the bonds which have higher yield to maturity than the trendlines. The average maturity of the bond portfolio is 2.48 years. Debt/EBITDA is an acceptable 5.96. Current yield is 6.63% with a yield to maturity of 9%.

A plot of Yield to maturity versus credit rating for recommended bonds labeled in blue is shown below.

A plot of Yield to Maturity versus Debt/EBITDA for recommended bonds labeled in blue is shown below.

These bonds would be a good starting point to build a high yield bond portfolio with acceptable risk.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tracking Bill Ackman's Pershing Square 13F Portfolio - Q3 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Pershing Square's 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Ackman's regulatory 13F form filed on 11/14/2025. Please visit our Tracking Bill Ackman's Pershing Square Holdings article for an idea on how his holdings have progressed over the years and our previous update for the fund's moves during Q2 2025.

Ackman's 13F portfolio value increased from ~$13.73B to ~$14.64B this quarter. The number of positions remained steady at 10. The portfolio remains heavily concentrated with a few huge bets. The top three positions account for ~59% of the total portfolio value: Uber Technologies, Brookfield Corp, and Alphabet.

Their flagship fund's (2004 inception) track record is excellent, with annualized returns of ~16% compared to ~10% for the S&P 500 index. YTD as of late November, they are up ~23%. In addition to partner stakes, the fund also invests the capital from Pershing Square Holdings (OTCPK:PSHZF), a public entity that debuted in Euronext Amsterdam in October 2014. This was set up primarily to increase the amount of capital invested that is permanent. It has always traded at a discount to NAV. It currently trades at $66.55 per share compared to an NAV of ~$87.

Note 1: Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) are other long positions in the partnership - the holdings were disclosed in 13D filings on November 15, 2013 - as they are not 13F securities, they are not listed in the 13F report. Ackman held just under 10% of the outstanding shares of both these businesses - 115.57M shares of FNMA at a cost basis of $2.29 and 63.5M shares of FMCC at a cost-basis of $2.14. The combined investment outlay was ~$400M. FNMA currently trades at $11.49 & FMCC at $10.85 per share. In March 2018, Pershing Square said their Fannie/Freddie pfds now amount to 21% of the total investment in the two GSEs. Their Q1 2025 investor call mentioned the following: Fannie/Freddie common shares could become an anchor for the sovereign wealth fund as they are uniquely situated as forever assets that can grow, given growth is the only way to solve the country's deficit problem. The low-30s price estimate provided in our January presentation upon successful exit from conservatorship may turn out to be low, given Pulte's operational, management, and board changes, which are solid steps toward running them as real private businesses rather than government mandated.

Note 2: Pershing Square has a ~6.2% ownership stake in UMG Music Group (OTCPK:UMGNF) at a cost-basis of ~€18 per share. It is down from ~10% as of Q4 2024, It currently trades at ~€21.48. It is still their largest equity stake. The position is not in their 13F report, as it is not US listed. Pershing Square said the following regarding the reduction in UMG: "UMG has substantially outperformed the rest of our portfolio (other than the GSEs) year to date in light of its extremely strong recent results. UMG was our largest position going into the day at more than ~27% of our capital. After the sale, it will still be our largest position at about ~17% of the portfolio."

Note 3: Pershing Square Tontine Holdings (PSTH), the liquidated SPAC, distributed SPARC shares (not tradeable currently) to previous PSTH common and warrant holders following its successful registration during Q3 2023.

Note 4: Pershing Square had a significant ownership stake in Nike (NKE) through deep-in-the-money calls. The position was fully disposed after the quarter ended.

To learn more about Bill Ackman, check out the book "Confidence Game: How Hedge Fund Manager Bill Ackman Called Wall Street's Bluff".

Stake Decreases

Uber Technologies (UBER): UBER is currently the top position in the 13F portfolio at 20.25%. It was purchased during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$61 and ~$82. The stock currently trades at $90.99. This quarter saw marginal trimming.

Note: Their cost-basis is in the high-60s. The investment thesis is based on the idea that ride hailing won't be a winner-takes-all business. In that context, Uber will have a central role to play.

Brookfield (BN): BN is now a large (top three) position at 19.21% of the portfolio. The stake was established during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$25 and ~$30. The bulk of the position was built during the next quarter at prices between ~$27 and ~$36. There was a ~18% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$33 and ~$42 The stock currently trades at $46.72. There was a marginal trimming this quarter.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the 3-for-2 stock-split in October.

Alphabet (GOOGL): GOOGL is now the third largest 13F stake at ~19% of the portfolio. It was purchased during March 2023 in the low-90s price range. There was a ~13% stake increase during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$104 and ~$128. That was followed by a ~20% increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$117 and ~$139. Q2 2024 saw a ~16% trimming at prices between ~$152 and ~$187. The stock currently trades at ~$318. There was a ~4% trimming this quarter.

Restaurant Brands International (QSR): The QSR stake is a large ~10% of the portfolio stake. Pershing Square's original cost-basis was ~$16. Q3 2017 saw a ~32% selling at prices between $59 and $66. That was followed with a ~22% reduction in H1 2018 at prices between $53 and $64. The four quarters through Q3 2019 also saw a ~28% selling at prices between $52 and $79. In June 2020, they increased the position by roughly two-thirds through forward contracts at $44.20 cost-basis. The stock currently trades at $71.93. There was marginal trimming this quarter.

Note: They own ~7.4% of the business.

Kept Steady:

Howard Hughes (HHH): HHH is now a 10.58% of the 13F portfolio position. The stake was first established in 2010 as a result of its spin-off from GGP Inc. The majority of the current stake is from the addition of ~10M shares in Q1 2020 at ~$50 per share through a private placement. Q1 2021 saw a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$78 and ~$102. Q4 2022 saw another ~17% stake increase at an average cost of ~$72 per share. Q4 2023 saw a ~12% stake increase at prices between ~$66 and ~$87. The stock is currently at $87.18.

Note: Regulatory filings show them owning 27.85M shares (46.9% of business) - in August, they acquired 9M shares of HHH at $100 per share and plans to transform the company into a diversified holding company with an insurance operation in the style of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A).

Amazon.com (AMZN): AMZN is a large 8.73% of the portfolio stake established during the last quarter at prices between ~$161 and ~$224. The stock currently trades at ~$229.

Note: Their overall cost-basis is $175 per share.

Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG): CMG is now a 5.77% of the portfolio position. The stake was established in Q3 2016 at a cost-basis of ~$8.10 per share. The position was sold down by ~30% in Q3 2018 in the high-single-digit price-range and that was followed with a ~17% trimming over the next four quarters. Q1 2020 also saw a one-third selling at ~$17.20 per share. There was a ~7% trimming in Q1 2021 at ~$26.80 per share. Q3 2021 saw a ~3% increase at ~$38.20 per share. There was a ~14% trimming over the two quarters through Q2 2023 at prices between ~$27.30 and ~$42.80. Q4 2023 saw a ~14% trimming at prices between ~$35.60 and ~$46.80. The next quarter also saw a ~10% trimming at prices between ~$44.30 and ~$59.10. Q2 2024 saw another ~22% reduction at prices between ~$57 and ~$69. That was followed by a ~14% selling during Q4 2024 at prices between $55.73 and $66.16. The next quarter saw a ~13% further reduction at prices between $47.55 and $61.16. The stock currently trades at $34.15.

Note: Pershing Square has fully exited this position after the quarter ended.

Hilton Worldwide Holdings (HLT): The 5.37% portfolio stake was established in October 2018. It was purchased at prices between $64 and $78. Q1 2020 saw a ~30% stake increase at a cost-basis of ~$70. Q1 2022 saw a ~20% selling at prices between ~$129 and ~$158. There was a ~7% trimming during Q1 2023 while Q3 2023 saw a ~10% increase. The next quarter saw a ~11% selling at prices between ~$147 and ~$182. There was a ~18% trimming during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$202 and ~$234. That was followed by a ~26% reduction during the next quarter at prices between $229.56 and $258.32. There was a ~45% selling during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$219 and ~$275. The stock currently trades at ~$274.

Note 1: Their overall cost-basis is ~$72 per share.

Note 2: In Q4 2018, Hilton Worldwide Holdings came back into the portfolio after a gap of eighteen months. The previous position was purchased in Q3 2016 and disposed of a year later. Pershing Square has said that the new position was acquired at a better valuation compared to their previous purchase. Also, the business structure has transformed into a capital-light model following the spinoff in early 2017 of Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) and Hilton Grand Vacations (HGV).

Hertz Global (HTZ): The 0.71% HTZ stake was established during Q1 2025 at prices between $3.26 and $4.74. The stock currently trades at $5.14.

Note: Their cost basis is ~$3.80, and their ownership stake is ~20%. They highlighted its asymmetric range of outcomes and several-fold return potential during their Q1 2025 investor call.

Seaport Entertainment (SEG): SEG was spun off from Howard Hughes in early August 2024. Shareholders received one share for every nine shares of HHH held. It started trading at ~$22 and currently goes for $21.38. There was a ~20% stake increase during Q4 2024 at prices between $26.12 and $33.86.

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Pershing Square's 13F stock holdings in Q3 2025:

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Adtalem Global: Solid Healthcare Education Platform, But Investigation Risk Limits Upside
data.description:
I have decided to follow-up Adtalem Global Education (ATGE). At the time of my last article, I rated the stock as a buy due to it's strong student base and clear growth. I am circling back to this company, because I have noticed quite a drop past month. Today the company is not a typical higher education business. This company transformed into a U.S. healthcare system component. The main focus is to supply the sector with constant flow of medical professionals. The sector is suffering from the deficit, which is quite deep and systemic. U.S. healthcare is experiencing the highest shortage in history. Yet this issue does not apply to the U.S. alone, my wife is a nurse, and trust me, my country has the same issue, if not bigger. The shortage in US is expected to reach more than 1.2 million of new specialists. In my opinion, this gap between supply and demand is not cyclical or temporary. This is a long-term structural issue, which is following demographic tendencies. In other words, the number of people that need healthcare services increases, while doctors and nurses suffer from professional burnout, also lets not forget to add high barrier of entry into studies and retirements each year.

Business Overview

In my opinion, ATGE is one of the biggest healthcare education services providers in US. They are positioning themselves as systematically important component in U.S. healthcare system and that is true in one way or another. Their business model is simple: provide career-oriented education. That means they are generating revenue from students and study programs.

Chamberlain University is where they teach nursing and healthcare management programs. In my opinion, it's going to be the main driver for a long time, unless recent shareholder inquiries reveal issues.

Walden University offers more programs, including healthcare, business, IT and psychology. They acquired this asset in 2020-2021 and it strengthened Adtalem's position in digital education, which was a very smart move.

Medicine and Veterinary schools consist of American University of the Caribbean School of Medicine, Ross University School of Medicine also Ross University School of Veterinary Medicine. These institutions are preparing doctors and veterinarians, which are high demand specialties.

Also, as I mentioned, ATGE is using hybrid learning. This lets them reach more students and give flexibility. Adding technology integration like AI programs, are strengthening their position too. Partnership with Google Cloud to create AI qualification program for healthcare students and specialists is a logical step. They understand that technology is not going anywhere. I believe in a long-run it's going to give them competitive advantage.

Financial Health and Growth

Let's look at the numbers. Revenue in Q1 FY2026 was $462.3 million, which is 10.8% more than a year ago. That is a good revenue growth, given that analysts were expecting lower (around $452.3 million). Revenue growth shows that demand for their programs remains strong. EPS was $1.67, compared to $1.18 a year ago. Adjusted EPS reached $1.75, and that is more than 35.7% growth compared to $1.29 a year ago. To me, it is very impressive result and it shows that they are managing costs and effectiveness.

Adjusted EBITDA margin expanded to 24.2%, compared to 23.2% a year ago and also shows that they are not only growing the revenue but increasing profitability.

Enrollment reached 97359, around 8% more than a year ago. And it marked ninth month when student numbers are growing, which in my opinion is very important in education business.

Chamberlain University enrollments grew 2.2% per year. Even though the growth might seem slower, it's still growth.

Walden University enrollments grew by 13.6% per year reaching the highest level in the university's history. And it's clear that Walden is successful, maybe because of their wider range of programs and digital education model.

Medicine and Veterinary segment grew by 2.4% per year, also showing stable growth, even though it is not that fast.

For FY2026 the company is expecting that annual revenue will reach $1.90 billion and that would mean 6% growth. Adjusted EPS is expected at $7.6-7.9 range, which would be 14-18.5% annual growth. I believe that these plans are quite ambitious and very possible given the current results we see.

Risks

In late October 2025 it was announced that DJS Law group initiated investor inquiry into Adtalem's possible violations of the securities laws. The investigation, according to the law firm, "focuses on whether the Company issued misleading statements and/or failed to disclose information pertinent to investors." Keep in mind this is not a formal regulatory investigation and does not imply that they are guilty, quite a normal process for lawyers to gather information on behalf of shareholders. Nevertheless share price fell almost 30%, which shows that the market is very sensitive to news like that. Such a situation, in my eyes, increases uncertainty and therefore I am more cautious as well. I will try to cover the stock whenever more information arises.

Let's say investigation finds that Chamberlain is really slowing, this would pose a new risk. The problems will expand into other segments and we might see corrected growth numbers, which will also lead to a sharp decline. Keep in mind that retail investors usually sell first, and only then read what happened.

Another risk, maybe not major like the main one is that a lot of students are depending on federal financial aid (Title IV). This means that Adtalem is sensitive to any federal financing changes, and securities fraud investigation is not going to help them in this case.

Summary

Even though Adtalem Global Education is showing strong financial results and operates in good market, the risk of securities fraud allegation is forcing me to be cautious until we see things clear. I believe that any investor should be aware of the situation, it is not about the growth currently buy about being clear on the situation, I will try to keep you posted regarding it. I will be re-rating this stock as hold until allegations clear up.

data.title: Beware Of The Unwinding Japanese Carry Trade
data.description:
On Wall Street, it is said of the carry trade that first they carry you in, and then they carry you out. By this, it is meant that a lot of money can be made by borrowing in a depreciating currency that has low interest rates and lending in an appreciating currency that has high interest rates.

The catch, however, was that you could lose your shirt if the currency in which you borrowed began to yield higher interest rates and started to appreciate. This would be especially the case at a time when the interest rates of the currency in which you lent started to decline, and that currency started to depreciate.

All of this would seem to be of utmost importance for the US and world financial markets today, given what is now going on in Japan. For years, as Japan struggled with deflation, beginning in 2016, the Bank of Japan pursued a yield control policy.

Under that policy, the Bank of Japan held its short-term lending rate at a negative level and aimed at keeping the 10-year Japanese government bond rate at zero percent.

Those low interest rates, together with the very much higher interest rates that were on offer in the rest of the world, encouraged global investors to borrow in yen and to finance their investment in other currencies.

Although estimates vary on the size of the Japanese carry trade, it is generally believed that it was at least some $500 billion. That would seem to have provided significant support to the world financial market rally.

The problem for world financial markets today is that at a time when the Federal Reserve and European Central Bank have been cutting interest rates, the Bank of Japan (BOJ) is now in the process of attempting to normalize its monetary policy.

It is being forced to do so both by the increase in Japanese inflation to around three percent, which is above the BOJ’s two percent inflation target, and by the expansionary budget policy now being pursued by Sanae Takaichi, the new Japanese prime minister.

The Bank of Japan is now signaling that later this month it will raise its interest rate at the very time when the Federal Reserve is expected to cut its interest rate next week by another 25 basis points.

The combination of a looser Japanese budget policy and the proposed normalization of the Bank of Japan’s monetary policy has led to a sharp rise in Japanese government bond rates to their highest level in the past twenty years.

Since the start of the year, both the 10-year and the 30-year Japanese government bond yields have increased by 100 basis points to their current levels of around 1.95 percent and 3.95 percent, respectively.

This has considerably increased the relative attractiveness of those bonds with respect to US government bonds, whose yields today are very little different than they were at the start of the year.

The challenge to world financial markets is that the narrowing of the interest rate spread between Japanese bonds and US bonds could lead to an unwinding of the Japanese carry trade and to the repatriation of capital to Japan by that country’s financial institutions.

That process could be reinforced by a meaningful appreciation of the Japanese yen, which is currently estimated to be undervalued by between 15 and 20 percent.

The prospective unwinding of the Japanese carry trade comes at an awkward time for the United States. It comes at the very time when Trump’s large tax cuts have increased the US government’s budget deficit to an annual rate of around $2 trillion.

The last thing that the US government needs is the selling of its bonds by one of its major creditors. That could contribute to a rise in US government bond yields, which could be a trigger for the bursting of the artificial intelligence bubble.

All of this would argue for a rethink of US budget policy with the aim of reducing the budget deficit and our reliance on the kindness of strangers to finance that deficit. Absent such a change, we should brace ourselves for some rough financial market sledding in the year ahead on the back of a meaningful rise in US Treasury bond yields.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Life After Mag 7
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Michael Kramer from Reading The Markets calls this market 'sideways with a lot of chop' (0:50). Life after Mag 7 (2:30). Healthcare names of interest (7:40). Robotics and AI (12:00). Life after government data (14:10). Startling bond environment (16:45).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Very excited to welcome back Michael Kramer, who writes under Mott Capital Management and who runs the investing group on Seeking Alpha called Reading The Markets. Welcome back to Investing Experts, Michael. Great to have you back on.

Michael Kramer: Good to be here.

Rena Sherbill: It's great to have you. So talk to us. We're at the beginning of December. You've been on a few times this year sharing your thoughts with the market, wondering if some bearishness is going to develop ever further, where there's been a lot of market volatility, a lot of questioning about where to go in this market and what this market even is.

How would you synthesize your thoughts these days?

Michael Kramer: I would say that the market has been sort of sideways with a lot of chop. I mean, really, since the beginning of October, we've seen volatility really begin to pick up some with really while just basically trading sideways, maybe there's a slightly upward tilt to it.

But I don't really know that there's much happening. It seems like the market is trying to go through this process of figuring out what the next move is. And I think there's a couple of different forces that are driving that right now. And one is obviously, you know, AI.

And I think we've spoke about this maybe over the summer. And it's funny because now the market is starting to think about some of the things that I believe we spoke about, which was

You one of my fears of is, the amount of spending that was going on for these data centers and AI. I think another piece is that liquidity in the market has obviously been a big factor over the years and the dynamics around that appear to be changing. And it's kind of made the Fed, it's brought the Fed and the balance sheet back into focus. And I think, you know, and

What the interesting thing, I guess, is that we're seeing, again, for the first time, really some pushback on AI and whether or not that's the theme that the market really wants to have going forward. And you're seeing some signs of rotation in the market, although I don't know if it's all good or bad types of rotation. But it really should be causing people to think about life after Mag-7.

Rena Sherbill (02:41.134)What is life after MAG7? Because that does seem to be the life that we're in right now. How do you see it continuing to develop given the fact that much of what you've discussed has or is now starting to come to pass or continuing to develop?

Michael Kramer: For the first time in this cycle, we've seen the credit market finally weigh in on Mag7 and AI tech. We've seen credit default swaps for Oracle go from very low 30s and 40 values to 120, 130 type values. You've seen them go up even for things like

CoreWeave (CRWV), which has been one of the AI names where it has a credit default swap that's now trading in the 700s, right? And these numbers all suggest probabilities of default over the next five years. So the fact that they're even rising, you know, tells you that the market is thinking about what's happening with all of this spending and it's starting to kind of make bets in some ways or look for protection in the event that something, you know, turns out that

Maybe some of these companies are making bad investment decisions. And that was something we didn't see previously. I mean, I can actually share my screen and show you. This is my LSEG workstation, and this is Oracle's (ORCL) credit default swap. And I'm just going to zoom out a little bit here. I mean, you can clearly see the movement in it, right? And this was around the time of the tariff tantrum. And this, of course, was during the period of time where the Fed was raising rates and tightening liquidity.

So this was something you saw basically in every single stock or credit default swap out there. In fact, we can compare it to just get a sense of what I'm trying to show here.

What I'm going to overlay here is called the CDX investment grade index and when you compare that with Oracle, just again to show that this period of time in here, this was a normal pattern that you were seeing across the market. Now this where we are. Where you're seeing a clear divergence in how the credit market is thinking about Oracle versus other investment grade type investments. And that's a clearly a different path that we're on right now.

And one reason why that's happening is because you're looking at companies like Oracle and Meta (META), where their free cash flows have on a trailing 12 month basis have rolled over, they're no longer going higher. They're going lower. And the more they spend on CapEx, the more they invest into AI, the more likely it is that those cash flows can continue to roll over if AI doesn't start producing meaningful new revenue and meaningful new earnings for these companies.

And so this gives me a sense that for the first time in this cycle, the market is starting to think about what could happen if these investments don't work out or if it turns out that the productivity enhancements that are likely to come from AI don't pay off anytime in the near term. And I think this is probably the greatest example I can show at this point.

But it's not just Oracle that this is happening to I could You know change it and put an Amazon which happens to be a stock that I own but regardless, I mean if we take Oracle out of the view You can see Amazon's (AMZN) CDS has also started to rise. I mean, it's nowhere near where Oracle's is but the fact that it's rising is something that is noteworthy and so again that's what I'm trying to reinforce, which is that for the first time, the market is questioning some of these businesses.

And I think what that means to me is that you could actually see a period of time, and you're seeing this a little bit now, where it's possible. Because of the way the S&P 500 (SP500) is constructed with this market cap weighting, you could actually see the S&P 500 go down being dragged by some of the bigger tech names while the rest of the index does reasonably well and outperforms.

What I've been thinking about and trying to really express to my subscribers actually not on a regular basis because our focus isn't sector rotations and things like that. But what I've been expressing a little bit over time has been this idea that it's time to maybe start thinking about other sectors of the market outside of AI and that could be healthcare.

Healthcare is one of those sectors that I'm the most excited about because to me that's where I think the biggest gains in AI could come from.

Rena Sherbill: You were sharing that last time you were on as well. Are they the same opportunities health care wise when you're looking at that sector that you've been thinking about for this past year?

Michael Kramer: It's kind of broadened out in some ways. And I don't remember the companies we spoke about at that time, but I think it was early summer. And so I probably had just bought UnitedHealth (UNH) for myself personally and for my portfolio.

And I really don't like to give personal, just know we're not giving personal investment advice here. And I'm not suggesting in any way or soliciting that you should be buying any of these things, but I'm trying to express some of the things that I've been looking at. UnitedHealthcare obviously was one of the ones that I mentioned.

I had bought shares of Zoetis (ZTS) also over the summer because they're an animal healthcare, animal vaccines, animal drugs. And one of the reasons why is because I own two dogs and treatments for dogs as they get older is very expensive. And so are some of these medications. And just looking at boxes of medications I'm giving to my dogs and knowing that the price on these medications has been rising over the years, Zoetis seemed like something that made a lot of sense to me from that standpoint.

But more recently, I've owned Illumina (ILMN) for some time as well, and I've owned Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) for some time also. So those aren't new positions, but it kind of gives a sense that I'm trying to rebuild my portfolio around these types of healthcare names.

And I recently completed my position in another company called Grail (GRAL), which was part of Illumina and was actually one of the reasons why I bought Illumina in 2023. Because I liked that they were working on this early detection test for cancer. And just recently they had come out with some results which looked favorable to me and knowing that that data was coming had prompted me to go out and finish the position off and building it to where I wanted it to be back in mid-September.

And that's another type of area where I think AI could be transformational, right? Because imagine being able to detect cancer six months earlier. In some cases, it could be life-saving.

The idea here being is going through the sequencing and having this ability to do things faster than maybe what we were able to do at previous points in time. So I'm looking for those types of things still.

I'm exploring things and thinking about things like Boston Scientific (BSX) and things in these more of these medical. Medical devices, Stryker (SYK), for example, makes parts for people, if you need a shoulder replacement, they might make it. They have an orthopedic robotic surgery machine that does hips.

There's just lots of different opportunities. And some of these stocks haven't performed nearly as well as the rest of the Mag 7 type names, which I've owned for years. I mean, I don't want to make it sound like I'm a negative person on Mag 7 because I've been in Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Amazon. I was involved in starting in 2022, but Alphabet I started with in 2017, Microsoft (MSFT) I started off with in 2018 and '19, and Apple (AAPL).

Those have been core holdings for me for a long time. And it was all built on the idea of the cloud and usage and dominance in the marketplace and how they just became everyday part of our lives.

And I saw that just increasing and the AI piece kind of came later, but I always knew that if there was going to be something with AI, those were going to be the companies that were going to be the leaders there anyway. But those positions I've also been telling my subscribers occasionally. Again, we don't really talk about this type of stuff on regular basis, but I've trimmed those positions rather significantly over the last year. I would say I've cut them all basically by half.

Rena Sherbill: What would you say, speaking of being early on tech and this AI development, what would you say about this announcement from the White House about accelerating their push into the robotics side of things? What would you say about that push? Will it meaningfully catalyze that sector of the industry?

Michael Kramer: I didn't read the news specifically and I so I didn't I can't really respond to that. I mean, robotics I think is something that could be important. I was looking at Teradyne (TER) for a long time just watching it and I could never find the time that I thought was right to buy it. And now it's gone up tremendously. So it totally would make no sense to me anymore. And again, the robotics portion was fairly a small part of their business.

The problem with the robotics part of the market is that I haven't really been able to find many publicly listed companies that are in robotics that trade here in the US. There's a few in Japan, but I'm not going to Japan to buy anything. And so that was one of the biggest struggles that I've had in that area.

(ISRG) is really one of the only ones that I've been able to find from a health care standpoint that makes anything like that. So I've struggled finding robotics, but I think robotics is important.

But the problem is right now is that so many of these stocks have gone up so much that if there is a broader correction in tech and AI, these robotic stocks would probably be hit just like the quantum stocks would be hit because they're all part of the same momentum trades.

And so I would shy away from looking at those things just because I think that they're part of that broader basket. I don't think that necessarily would fare any better in an environment if we were to see a move away from AI.

Rena Sherbill: And taking the broader picture into more of a context as it pertains to the market, what would you say the jobs data, what kind of effect is the jobs data having on things? What kind of effect are these interest rate conversations having in effect? How much is that a part of your analysis and how much is that playing a part of your thinking these days?

Michael Kramer: You know, the funniest thing I was telling some I was telling a friend of mine that the greatest thing that probably happened from my viewpoint over the last month or two was that there was no government data.

Because it gave me an opportunity to really watch the market in a way that I hadn't been able to do before, which was a market that was moving on no economic news on a regular basis.

And I began to realize that although in my writings, I've written about volatility and option positioning and all the other stuff, it really crystallized to me how much the market just brushes all of that really aside. And it's not that the economic data itself, the market positions for, and then what you see is the post-positioning knee-jerk reaction when you get the move.

So the economic data, the way I think about it now, sets up the positioning. And then once the news comes out, you get that unwinding of that trade. And so not having the data really allowed me to kind of hone in specifically to take that filter, that noise out that we get, and see more closely how the market structure has evolved let's say over the last four or five years.

And while I think the Fed plays a important role in liquidity and rates and all this economic data matters, the way that it's interpreted by people watching is not necessarily correct, not realizing how much positioning around these events drives that outcome in terms of how the market moves. And so

While I think these pieces of data matter a lot for things like bonds and currencies, I don't know that the interpretations that are received post economic data are necessarily the correct ones anymore.

Rena Sherbill: Anything you want to say about the bonds and the currencies?

Michael Kramer: I think the most startling thing to me is really what's happening in bonds.

First of all, I mean,we talk about rates going down, but I think the funny thing is, or the odd thing, is that no matter how much the Fed has cut, and the Fed's cut a pretty decent amount already, and we know that they're planning to cut more, but it seems like everyone has missed the fact that the 10-year rate doesn't go down.

The 30-year rate hasn't gone down. The 30-year rate is still trading around 475, and it's just basically 25 or 30 basis points away from being back at the highs it was at not that long ago. And even on the 10-year rate, it's only 50 to 75 basis points away. I mean, in the market today, that could move very quickly if it were ever to revert.

With the terrible ADP jobs data we got today, you didn't really see a very big reaction in the bond market from it. And I think that's a really important and telling thing. It's telling us that the market that, and I've kind of theorized this again with my subscribers for a long time. I think 4% on the 10 year is the floor for it.

And the reason why I think it's the floor is because the curve is simply too flat. I'll pull up my chart again. If we look at a chart of the 10 year minus the three year, the 10 year minus the three year is only separated right now by 33 basis points. That's it.

Why would anyone want to go out and buy a 10-year bond when they can just keep rolling over three-month treasury bills on a regular rate, on a regular time scale, and only be losing 33 basis points, avoiding all that risk that comes associated with owning a longer duration bond? This yellow line marks the zero bound. Now, I want to show you something.

If we go on a weekly chart now, this is going to show us where the curve is on a historical basis. We're all the way over here. Notice that the curve typically peaks around 385 to 400 basis points above. The 10-year normally peaks 385 to 400 basis points above three-month treasury rate.

If we use the Fed funds rate as a proxy for where the three-month treasury rate will be, mean, let's just say the three-month, the Fed funds rate bottom somewhere around 3%, which is what the market is pricing in right now.

So if the Fed funds rate bottoms at 3 % and the 10-year, and we use the three-month as a proxy, it means that the three-month would probably also be around 3%, which is 70 basis points, that would still only put the spread at around 100 basis points. So unless the Fed is going back to zero, the curve is too flat, even if the Fed cuts.

And so this is why I think bond yields at the back of the curve haven't gone down nearly as much as people think they're going to go down just because the Fed is cutting. Here's the 30 year minus the three. And it's really the same thing. It typically peaks nearly 450 basis points above Fed funds, above the three month treasury bill. again, like if they cut another 75 basis points, that's going to put this somewhere around 175. You're still only halfway there.

At a 3% Fed funds rate and a 3 % Treasury bill rate, you would think that the 30 year would be closer to 7%. And I think that's why you don't see rates going down at the back of the curve, mostly because the market is basically not sure exactly where the Fed ends up cutting.

Because right now, the way this thing stand, the curve is too flat for where we are in this cycle. And my guess would be is that we're likely to see, and we're not talking about post-GFC. We're not talking, these values aren't all pre-GFC. This is 2011. This is 2014. Rates were at zero. That meant that you had a 30-year rate in 2014 at 388, 389, basically 4%.

So again, if the Fed's going to stop cutting at three, then the 30 years should probably be closer to seven. If the Fed's going to stop cutting at two, then the 30 years should still be somewhere around six. What this tells me is that this is the reason why you're not seeing rates on the back of the curve going down.

The other interesting thing is that when you look at the US five year minus the Japanese five year, this is also fascinating to me because what you do is you take then the Japanese yen and you overlay it with this chart and you basically see a really huge divergence which is forming right now, right in here. And what this is suggesting, if you just kind of zero in on this part of the chart, is that basically the Japanese yen has been weakening and strengthening versus the dollar based on the movements and the changes in the interest rate differentials.

And right now what's happening is just giant divergence. The yen, despite the spread shrinking, is weakening versus the dollar (USD:JPY). The yen really should, based on this chart over the last, let's call it five years, the yen should be somewhere, call it around 130, not somewhere around 155. And so that's telling you a message too.

The message it's telling you is either rates, five-year rates, 10-year rates are too low in the US, and they need to go higher. It's telling you that the Japanese yen is too weak, or it's telling you the Japanese yen is too weak, or it needs to strengthen. The third option would be is that Japanese rates are too high, and they need to come down.

I don't think Japanese rates are too high because the BOJ is likely going to be raising rates, and they have a 3% inflation problem. I don't think that, and I don't know if the yen is undervalued or overvalued because I don't know where US rates are going to go.

So if you combine the steepness of the yield curve with where the FX market is, it would suggest to me that US rates should be higher, not lower on the back of the curve because it's too flat currently. That was a lot to digest, I'm sure.

Rena Sherbill: You talked about, you know, the Bank of Japan telegraphy and a rate increase later this month. And earlier this week, we saw this big rise in Japanese government bond yields that we hadn't seen since, speaking of the GFC, since 2008.

What are you expecting to happen at the December Fed meeting? What does this news out of Japan, what do you think it portends for the US? And what do you think it will mean for investors moving forward as we end the year and maybe even into 2026 as well?

Michael Kramer: I mean, in theory, right? Because it seems like we're living in a really strange time where things are broken and detached from what the norm used to be. mean, rising rates in Japan sort of remove the low point anchor in the world.

Meaning that when a Japanese two-year was trading at negative 12, because they had negative interest rates, you could see US rates lower, right? Because everything moves a little bit together. You can see that there is some movement that happens together. So right now, if you're seeing rates in Japan rising and basically in order to keep the spreads from completely going haywire, then it would mean to me that rates in the US, rates everywhere in the world are going to be somewhat elevated relative to where they had been before.

Because again, if you look at the spread, if we look at the Japanese five-year and a US five-year, you can see how they've gone together. But I think what's more important is if you look at this spread, the spread has been fairly consistent. It's not like it's been between 40 basis points at the low and as high as four and a half basis points at the high.

But the point is, if Japan kept raising rates and the US rate kept going down, you wouldn't want to be in a scenario where this rate flipped or something.

That's what you're seeing actually happening right now in Italy compared to Germany, which is interesting because this this is creating big distortions too. So you can see how the rates in Germany and Italy tended to go together. But if you notice more now, they're coming together. Notice that the rate in Italy is falling while the rate in Germany is kind of flat.

And this is actually a really important thing. It again demonstrates the changes and really the political instabilities that are going over and dramatic shifts that we're getting in Europe. The spread has fallen to just 70 basis points, meaning that an Italian tenure today is only seven basis points above a German versus where it was during, remember, the euro crisis, the debt crisis.

Remember, at one point, the Italian tenure was trading 500 basis points above the German tenure. Basically, what's happened is that the German tenure is no longer viewed as, based on this chart alone, is no longer viewed as safe as it used to be.

And we'll say, well, what do I care about what's happening between Italian and German spreads? Well, I can tell you why you want to care about it, because it affects what's happening here in the US too.

This is a chart of financial conditions. And you can see that basically ebbs and flows and financial conditions here in the US go with the spreads. But also more importantly, if I just kind of shimmy things around here a little bit, is that if you go to some of these high yield spreads that we were just talking about, this is the high yield index option adjusted spread.

And then you add in the spread with Italy and Germany. And I just make that regular. Again, you can see how they kind of go together. And let me just put them on. So all of these things, I feel like matter a great deal.

And so if, for example, you suddenly see rates in Japan going up and rates in the US going down, and those spreads really start converging too much, it could lead to the yen actually strengthening too much versus a dollar. And that would be something the DOJ doesn't want. And so the idea here being is that if Japan is raising rates and rates in Japan are going up, I would expect the curve around the world to rise along with it.

Rena Sherbill: What else would you add to this conversation? What else do you feel like is important for investors to be keeping in mind right now?

Michael Kramer: It seems to me like something is happening in the market and the backdrop has changed because of what I said at the beginning with the credit spreads, because of the fact that you're seeing rates in Japan rising, the carry trade is basically going away. And that's been a source of liquidity for the marketplace as well.

And so I think the main takeaway is that we need to start really being focused on other things than Mag 7, AI, and big tech. Because the thing that I learned the most in the year 2000 isn't that the internet wasn't revolutionary, because obviously it was. But the companies that were the leaders in that bubble period didn't come out as the leaders.

Whoever has heard of Yahoo, right? I mean, we've heard of it. But the company has been irrelevant for two decades, right? But they were, know, AOL. These companies were all sort of the leaders then. They're no longer really relevant today. And so it really will be more exciting to me to find the leaders of tomorrow and that the leaders of today may not necessarily be the ones that lead the next leg of the market higher, whenever that should occur.

Rena Sherbill: Well, I still know somebody who knows somebody who knows somebody that has a Yahoo email address.

Michael Kramer: Yeah, well, I know some people too that have AOL accounts also.

Rena Sherbill: There you go. There's no better time to take advantage because we are having a 20% off site wide sale, including Michael's investing group called Reading The Markets. You can find more of his writings under Mott Capital Management on Seeking Alpha.

I also wanted to point out that we are now posting clips - Michael was talking about some charts. Go to our YouTube channel, that's youtube.com/seekingalpha, and you can soon find and see some of the clips and some of the charts that Michael has so graciously shared with us today.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lennar: Recovery Hopes Overly Optimistic (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Lennar Corporation (LEN) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing over 20% of their value. Most of those losses occurred late last year as the Fed temporarily paused its rate-cutting cycle and mortgage rates stayed stubbornly high. As a result, housing market activity has remained muted throughout 2025. I last covered shares of Lennar in June when I rated the stock a “Hold,” which was too conservative given shares have gained about 25% since then as the Federal Reserve has begun cutting interest rates, sparking hopes of a bottom in housing activity.

Looking first at the macro environment, homebuilder stocks have gained some momentum as we have seen interest rates decline. While much attention is paid to Fed policy, longer-term yields are more relevant for the pricing of 30-year mortgages, and we have also seen 10-year Treasury yields move lower. As you can see below, rates have spent much of the past three years in the 6.5%-7.25% range. We have more recently broken below this to ~6.25%.

We did this briefly last year when the Fed began cutting rates, but it proved short-lived. Furthermore, we are nearing the 6.0% level, which I have viewed as a key level to improve affordability and bring hesitant buyers off the sideline. Frankly, I am cautious about mortgage rates falling much further from here, given persistent Treasury deficits, rising corporate borrowing to fund AI data centers, and my view that the United States will avoid recession, meaning the Fed is likely to do less, not more, than is currently priced in.

Even as national home prices are flattish over the past year, there has been meaningful regional divergence. As you can see below, 195 of the 300 largest markets have price gains, outnumbering those with price declines. That said, the South (especially Florida and Texas) is materially underperforming. These are the markets that have seen the most construction and appear to be struggling with excess supply as post-COVID migratory trends have slowed.

While national real estate statistics are important, real estate is really a local asset. Lennar, like most of the builders, has outsized exposure to the South (hence why these markets have the most supply issues). A flat national market where FL is down and NY is up is worse for its profitability than a flat national market where FL is rising and NY is falling. In fact, Florida and Texas are well over 30% of the business, and I do not expect the Sun Belt to see prices rise before 2027.

Now, one positive is that homebuilders are steadily pulling back, given weaker pricing and higher financing costs. As you can see below, the number of homes under construction has been falling steadily for several years, though it remains above pre-COVID levels. All else equal, lower supply should support pricing, which is why I do not expect home prices to decline by more than 3% nationally. With weak housing starts, I expect supply to continue falling at least through mid-2026.

This environment has proven to be challenging for Lennar, and the company frankly misjudged the duration of the downturn in my view, forcing it to become extremely promotional. In the company’s fiscal third quarter, Lennar earned $2.00, which missed expectations by $0.09 as revenue fell by 6% to $8.8 billion. While discussing Q3 results, management noted that it was difficult to maintain sales activity, and the company was forced to increase incentives to keep inventory from growing too high.

For much of 2025, LEN was chasing volumes and being more aggressive on incentives to move homes. This helped maintain some economies of scale and positioned it for upside if the market recovered. With no clear upturn and margins coming under pressure, the company appears to be backing away from this strategy, pulling back sales expectations and trying to stabilize margins. During the quarter, it delivered 21,584 homes, which was a bit below expectations, reflecting the limits of its incentive strategy.

In fact, sales incentives were 14.3% and were 100bps above target. As a result, margins compressed to 17.5%, down 500bps from last year. SG&A was 8.2% of revenue, leaving a net margin of 9.2%. SG&A was up 150bps from last year, given the weaker revenue environment.

While its backlog has 9 more units than last year, its value is down by over $1 billion to $6.6 billion, given a $65k drop in the average selling price. This speaks to the meaningful softening in its core markets and reliance on incentives to sustain sales. The company has also shifted down-market via acquisition, leaving it more exposed to first-time buyers who face the most challenging affordability situation.

Importantly, Lennar maintains an excellent balance sheet. Debt to capital is just 13.5%, and this metric could comfortably run in the mid-20% area. As such, Lennar can return capital to shareholders. During the quarter, it bought back $507 million of stock. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 5.5% from last year. Shares also offer a 1.5% dividend yield.

Aside from financial results, in November, Lennar announced one of its co-CEOs would be retiring at the end of the year. Its other co-CEO, Stuart Miller, who is also chairman, will remain in his position, and the company will return to the traditional position of having a sole CEO. I do not expect this change to cause any material shift in operating results or strategy.

Lennar completed the divestment of its stake in Millrose Properties (MRP) in November. It spun out MRP earlier this year; MRP has Lennar’s legacy land holdings, and this move reduced the capital intensity of Lennar’s balance sheet. LEN exchanged its last 20% stake in MRP for LEN shares, allowing it to retire ~8 million shares. This reduces its share count by about 3%.

Looking ahead, the company now expects to deliver 22,000-23,000 homes in Q4 with a 17.5% gross margin. As such, it expects to earn $2.10-$2.30. Back in June, I thought Lennar could potentially earn $10. Instead, it looks likely to earn $8-$8.50 in 2025, as margin pressures have been much larger than I initially expected given the relative underperformance in its key markets.

Currently, analysts expect the company to earn about $9 next year. I struggle to see the company earning much more than this, as I expect deliveries to remain fairly muted and similar to this year’s level. Additionally, we likely will see more tariff costs next year, given the full-year impact of tariffs on lumber and other building materials. I view the homebuilding sector as likely to have particular difficulty in passing these costs on to consumers. As a result, even if it can reduce incentives, recapturing meaningful margin will likely be difficult before 2027.

With $8.50-$9.00 of earnings, shares are trading over 14x. The homebuilding sector has spent several years stuck in the 8-12x range, and this multiple expansion points to investors increasingly betting on a recovery in margins and activity. I fear we are likely to see more of the same in the coming months, with a material recovery more than 12 months away. As such, I am moving shares to a “Sell” after their recent outperformance. I would struggle to pay more than 12x, especially given its weak margin performance, and target a return towards $110.

Unless we see mortgage rates continue to fall and pierce 6%, I fear the recent rally is more of a fake-out than a sustained move to the upside, similar to what we saw last year. Plus, if rates do fall further, but that is because the labor market rolls over, that scenario is unlikely to be a net positive for the housing sector. As such, I view now as a prudent time to take advantage of recent gains and exit LEN.

data.title: International Equity Annual Returns: Non-Correlated To The U.S. Returns And Don't Forget Japan
data.description:
(h/t Mike Zaccardi on X)

Per this bar chart, European equity is having its best year vs. the US since 2006. European equities are being driven by the big banks and the anti-immigration policies of Italy and Germany, not to mention the US mandating that NATO commit 5-6% of GDP to NATO defense after previously relying on the generosity of America to foot what is thought to be about 70% of the NATO defense bill. (Source: Google and Grok).

International Annual Returns:

Source: Internal spreadsheet – Morningstar annual returns

The most compelling aspect of the international return data is that the 5, 10 and 15-year returns are still mostly single digits. The underperformance of international since just prior to the 2008 Great Financial Crisis has been material.

Japan

In 2025, this blog started buying the iShares MSCI Japan ETF (EWJ) for some clients. There are worries over the “yen carry trade” bubbling up again, but even with the Japan 10-year note trading up to 1.97% today, the Japanese stock market seems to have taken the end of the so-called trade in stride.

It was last August ’24 when the trade bubbled up one weekend, got everyone’s attention in the Sunday night futures markets, and the US markets opened sharply lower that Monday morning, only to have the worries dissipate within a day or two.

It’s a personal opinion, but after 36 years of a deflationary economy with the Nikkei not making a new high during that time, the fact that the Nikkei has now broken out and the Japanese stock market is absorbing higher interest rates might be signaling to investors that the old worries are over. Again, that is one opinion.

Japan’s new prime minister and the fact that she is very cautious on China might move Japan closer to the US on trade policy. My understanding of the whole 1980s Japanese economic miracle was that the huge trade surpluses generated by Japan (but Japan closed off their domestic markets to any serious competition) forced all that yen into a gigantic real estate bubble that led to 35 years of a domestic deflationary economy and zero interest rates. While that summary is probably greatly simplified, hopefully readers get the point.

Summary/conclusion

In addition to the above mutual funds and ETFs being tracked, this blog should start a single-country ETF spreadsheet with annual returns, but I should also eat less sugar and exercise more, so we know how that will go.

The point is (some) international has seen a lamp lit underneath it. Looking at longer-term annual returns, the 5, 10 and 15-year international returns are still attractive relative to the S&P 500 and large-cap US indices. Japan is very interesting after a 35-year bear market in the Nikkei.

Just like in the late 1990s, we have to assume that the tech trade, the Mag 7, the Nasdaq Comp, and the QQQ trade will end at some point, and perhaps not quietly.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All return data is sourced from Morningstar. Readers should gauge their own comfort with portfolio volatility and adjust accordingly. It’s likely in 2026 these “annual return” posts will be done less frequently, like every 2 or 3 months, rather than monthly. None of this information may be updated, and if updated, may not be done on a timely basis.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Buy Acadia Pharmaceuticals For Daybue/Nuplazid Stability And Pipeline Optionality
data.description:
Introduction

My last write-up on Acadia Pharmaceuticals Inc. (ACAD) was in March 2024. I was Buy on its stock, and ACAD has mirrored the index gains during this time.

Recall that the company was busy protecting NUPLAZID’s IP. Moreover, Acadia’s newer drug, DAYBUE, was showing some early signs of trouble in the Rett syndrome market, with many questioning DAYBUE’s tolerability profile. The company attributed DAYBUE’s early 2024 script stagnation to “seasonal” noise.

As is common in this sector, a lot has changed over the past year-plus. Some good, some bad.

The article that follows evaluates Acadia in light of recent developments. While peak expectations of DAYBUE are meaningfully lower, it and NUPLAZID offer a safety net ahead of key pipeline readouts, meriting an ongoing bullish stance.

NUPLAZID

Acadia’s flagship drug, NUPLAZID, for Parkinson’s disease psychosis, generated $609.4 million last year (up 11% Y/Y). Q3 2025 net product revenue was $177.5 million (up 12.5% Y/Y), and the company is guiding for $685 million to $695 million for FY25.

Recall that NUPLAZID was first approved in 2016, so the fact that it is still steadily growing >10% Y/Y is really encouraging. That said, 2016 was a long time ago, right, highlighting NUPLAZID’s IP vulnerability.

On that front, NUPLAZID’s IP position has improved. In June, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit confirmed the validity of the key NUPLAZID patent, rejecting an obviousness-type double patenting challenge by generic manufacturer, MSN. This effectively gives NUPLAZID composition-of-matter protection into 2030. Any protection beyond that is buoyed by “weaker” (legally speaking) patents, like a formulation patent on the 34 mg capsule that could extend protection to 2038. At least Acadia has the comfort in knowing that its main product (that generates ~2/3 of total sales) is safe for at least another few years.

DAYBUE

DAYBUE ended up generating $348.4 million in 2024. Recall that management had to cut guidance in 2024 to roughly $340 million and $350 million. In Q3 2025, DAYBUE net product sales were up 11% Y/Y to $101.1 million. Acadia guides for DAYBUE revenue to fall between $385 million and $400 million in FY25.

While DAYBUE’s launch was undoubtedly rocky, it has successfully transitioned to a durable, moderately beating-the-market rare-disease franchise with global upside. Canada approved the drug in late 2024, and Acadia has its eyes set on the EU in 2026.

During its earnings call, management cited persistency “above 50% at 12 months.” While not disastrous, this is clearly capping the true steady-state base. Meanwhile, Canada's reimbursement was described as “disappointing,” and the EU is framed cautiously. So, it’s apparent that ex-U.S. growth is unlikely to provide a big boost for DAYBUE.

Pipeline

Here’s where things get a bit tricky for Acadia. The company is pushing a pipeline-build strategy and assembling multiple “shots-on-goal.”

ACP-204 is probably Acadia's most interesting drug candidate. It is a “next-gen” 5-HT2A inverse agonist (same target class as NUPLAZID) that attempts to improve upon NUPLAZID’s safety issues. Like many antipsychotics, NUPLAZID carries a label warning for QT-interval prolongation. ACP-204 is designed to eliminate the QT signal and act faster. It is currently in a Phase 2/3 program (RADIANT) in Alzheimer’s disease, or AD, psychosis, and a Phase 2 trial in Lewy body dementia psychosis.

The AD psychosis pursuit is an interesting one because NUPLAZID explicitly failed here. Recall that the FDA wanted to see more AD-specific evidence. Importantly, Acadia has learned from NUPLAZID trial shortcomings. RADIANT evaluates AD psychosis only (not mixed dementia). There are no drugs approved specifically for AD psychosis, and off-label solutions carry boxed mortality warnings in dementia.

So, I think ACP-204 has a legit shot here, despite the past 5-HT2A inverse agonist mixed signals in AD psychosis. This market is, arguably, at least twice the size of Parkinson’s psychosis.

However, in September, Acadia reported a negative Phase 3 outcome for ACP-101 in Prader-Willi Syndrome. While this was Acadia's most advanced drug, it wasn’t its most important one, judging from its stock’s muted reaction following the data.

Other, earlier-stage drugs include ACP-211 (deuterated R-norketamine) in Phase 1 for TRD/MDD, ACP-711 (selective GABA_A-a3 modulator) for essential tremor (Phase 2-ready), and ACP-2591 (cGP analogue) for Rett and Fragile X (Phase 1).

Financial Health

I’ve pulled up Acadia's income statement. Net product sales were $278.633 million in Q3. Impressively, SG&A expenses remained unchanged while revenues saw an 11% Y/Y increase. That said, Acadia is expanding DAYBUE’s field force in the U.S. and Europe, so don’t flat SG&A to last. The company is clearly funneling more cash towards R&D (from $66.6 million in Q3 2024 to $87.83 million in Q3 2025). Net income more than doubled Y/Y to $71.78 million. The company is comfortably profitable.

The balance sheet looks solid. The company ended the quarter with $847 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investment securities, and no major long-term debts.

Final Words

Acadia has built a nice CNS-related business on the pillars of DAYBUE and NUPLAZID. Although DAYBUE isn’t likely to become the blockbuster that some, including myself, envisioned before its launch, it is, like NUPLAZID, a steady compounder.
