Long-term, especially post-2030, Acadia will need some pipeline victories for its stock to take the “next step up.” ACP-204 does represent a meaningful “shot-on-goal,” and I suspect that a clean win (Phase 2 data expected mid-2026) would propel Acadia's stocks to levels not seen since early 2021 (before it was apparent that NUPLAZID wasn’t going to capture the dementia-related psychosis market).

All in all, ACAD offers a steady floor in NUPLAZID/DAYBUE with serious upside potential with ACP-204, meriting ongoing speculation (“Buy”).

data.title: Initial Unemployment Claims Drop To 3-Year Low
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Initial jobless claims measure the number of people who file for unemployment for the first time in a given week. In the week ending November 29th, initial jobless claims were at a seasonally adjusted level of 191,000, the lowest level in over three years.

This represents a decrease of 27,000 from the previous week's figure and was lower than the forecast of 219,000. Initial jobless claims have fallen for four straight weeks.

Here is the complete data series dating back to 1967. There is a good bit of volatility in this indicator, which is why the four-week moving average is a more useful number than the weekly data because it smooths short-term fluctuations to highlight the overall trend. The four-week moving average currently stands at 214,750, down 9,500 from the previous week and the lowest level since January.

Outside of the COVID spike, initial unemployment claims have never been greater than 700,000 for a given week, making the chart above less useful for identifying trends. To address this, we've adjusted the y-axis on the chart below to provide a closer view of the data, minimizing the impact of the COVID surge.

Notice the relationship between recessions and the rise in weekly unemployment claims. To no surprise, the 4-week moving average begins to rise at or before the start of a recession and peaks around its conclusion.

In another attempt to eliminate the COVID spike, the chart below shows initial unemployment claims starting in October 2021.

Initial Unemployment Claims: Not Seasonally Adjusted

The headline unemployment insurance data - and the charts above - are seasonally adjusted. But what does the non-seasonally adjusted data look like? The chart below highlights its extreme volatility, as shown by the green dots. The four-week moving average helps reveal the recurring seasonal patterns, such as the regular spikes in January.

Because of the extreme volatility of the non-adjusted weekly data, we can add a 52-week moving average to give a better sense of the secular trends. The chart below also has a linear regression through the data (red line), which we are currently below.

Continuing Unemployment Claims

Continuing unemployment claims are the number of people who have already filed for unemployment and have continued to claim benefits for a subsequent week. Unlike initial unemployment claims, continuing claims are not considered a leading economic indicator and are a less important measure of economic health.

Additionally, data for continuing claims has a one-week lag compared to initial claims. In the week ending November 22nd, continuing jobless claims were at a seasonally adjusted level of 1,939,000, one of the highest levels in over four years. This represents a decrease of 4,000 from the previous week's figure and was lower than the forecast of 1,960,000.

Here is the data series dating back to 1967. Again, we've included the 4-week moving average to highlight the overall trend. Continuing jobless claims have hovered near multi-year highs for the past several months.

Here is a zoomed-in view so that the COVID spike is not as prominent.

And lastly, here is a chart starting in November 2021 to see the more recent trends in continuing claims.

Unemployment Claims as a Recession Indicator

For an analysis of unemployment claims as a percent of the labor force, see this regularly updated piece, Unemployment Claims as a Recession Indicator.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
